TreeMapChart.heroTitle: Free Online Tree Map Chart Maker
TreeMapChart.heroDescription: Free online tree map chart maker. Compare part-to-whole values with nested rectangles, customize labels and color, and preview the layout instantly.
TreeMapChart.whyItWorksDescription: A tree map turns large category lists into a compact area chart that makes size differences obvious without scrolling through a long table.
TreeMapChart.comparisonPoint1: See which categories dominate the total by comparing rectangle area instead of scanning many rows of numbers.
TreeMapChart.comparisonPoint2: Fit more categories into one view than a regular bar chart when dashboard space is limited.
TreeMapChart.comparisonPoint3: Use the layout to highlight concentration, long-tail categories, and relative contribution at a glance.
TreeMapChart.productHighlightsTitle: Built for quick input, live preview, and lightweight tree map customization.
TreeMapChart.feature1Description: Rename categories and update values directly in the editor without leaving the chart workflow.
TreeMapChart.feature2Description: Adjust numbers and immediately see how the rectangle sizes rebalance in the preview area.
TreeMapChart.feature3Description: Toggle category labels, value labels, and tooltips based on how much detail you want to show.
TreeMapChart.feature4Description: Change the chart title, metric label, and theme color while keeping the editor usable on desktop and mobile.
TreeMapChart.useCasesTitle: Use it for budget mix, portfolio share, market segmentation, and category-heavy reports.
TreeMapChart.useCase1: Show how a total budget is allocated across teams, products, or cost centers.
TreeMapChart.useCase2: Compare website traffic share by channel, campaign, or content group.
TreeMapChart.useCase3: Visualize sales contribution by product line, brand, or region in one compact view.
TreeMapChart.useCase4: Summarize portfolio, inventory, or asset composition where share matters more than trend.
TreeMapChart.useCase5: Highlight which departments or categories dominate a limited resource pool.
TreeMapChart.useCase6: Present long-tail category distributions without stacking many bars into one chart.
TreeMapChart.decisionGuide1Point1: Your main question is how much each category contributes to the total.
TreeMapChart.decisionGuide1Point2: You need to fit many categories into a compact dashboard area.
TreeMapChart.decisionGuide1Point3: Readers care more about relative size than exact axis positions.
TreeMapChart.decisionGuide1Point4: You want to reveal concentration, dominance, or imbalance across categories.
TreeMapChart.decisionGuide2Point1: You need precise comparisons between close values and a bar chart is easier to read.
TreeMapChart.decisionGuide2Point2: You want to show change over time and a line chart fits better.
TreeMapChart.decisionGuide2Point3: You only have a few categories and a regular bar chart is simpler.
TreeMapChart.decisionGuide2Point4: You need to emphasize sequence, ranking order, or exact axis values instead of area.
TreeMapChart.faqTitle: Everything people usually ask before building a tree map.
TreeMapChart.faq1Question: What is a tree map chart best for?
TreeMapChart.faq1Answer: A tree map chart is best for showing part-to-whole relationships when you have many categories and want to compare their relative size in one compact view.
TreeMapChart.faq2Question: How is a tree map different from a bar chart?
TreeMapChart.faq2Answer: A bar chart compares categories along a shared axis, while a tree map compares them by area. Tree maps save space, but bar charts are usually better for precise comparisons.
TreeMapChart.faq3Question: Can I use a tree map for percentages?
TreeMapChart.faq3Answer: Yes. Tree maps work well with raw values or percentages as long as each value represents part of a larger whole.
TreeMapChart.faq4Question: When does a tree map become hard to read?
TreeMapChart.faq4Answer: It becomes harder to read when you have too many tiny categories, very similar values, or labels that need exact ordering and precise comparison.
TreeMapChart.faq5Question: Should the values add up to a total?
TreeMapChart.faq5Answer: Usually yes. A tree map is most meaningful when each rectangle represents a share of the same overall total.
TreeMapChart.faq6Question: Why use this tool instead of a spreadsheet chart?
TreeMapChart.faq6Answer: This tool focuses on fast data entry, instant preview, and lightweight customization so you can build a clean tree map without a heavier spreadsheet workflow.
TreeMapChart.ctaTitle: Build a tree map in minutes.
TreeMapChart.ctaDescription: Enter your category values, adjust labels and color, and use the live preview above to turn a dense table into a compact part-to-whole chart.
TreeMapChart.editorSingleColorDescription: All blocks use shades of the theme color.
TreeMapChart.editorColorfulDescription: Each block uses a different color.
TreeMapChart.editorEmptyState: Add at least one positive value to render the tree map.
